entities.name: vāk
entities.cite: however it is set going by the utterance

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: for it is by utterance that the object is determined

entities.name: rudhi
entities.cite: just so does it become established

entities.name: mātrā
entities.cite: Whether as its own measure or as the measure of another

entities.name: vikarā
entities.cite: the contour settles along the lines speech first draws

axioms.name: vidhiyate
axioms.cite: for the object is *ordained* by utterance

axioms.name: characterizes
axioms.cite: the contour settles along the lines speech first draws, and repeated saying is what fixes it

denials.name: nitya_akara
denials.cite: Objects do not first possess determinate contours which speech then reports

denials.name: paurvapya
denials.cite: the contour settles along the lines speech first draws

claims.name: rudhatameti
claims.cite: just so does it become established

claims.name: vidhiyate
claims.cite: by utterance that the object is determined

rejected_readings.label: object_preexistence
rejected_readings.rendering: Objects possess determinate contours before being reported by speech, which merely describes existing objective realities.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the commentary's explicit statement that objects do not pre-exist with determinate form; speech does not report pre-existing contours but creates them through utterance.

rejected_readings.label: speech_as_passive
rejected_readings.rendering: The utterance merely reflects or passively conveys objects that are independently established through other means.
rejected_readings.why: Treats vak as a passive mirror rather than the active determinant (vidhiyate) that the verse emphasizes. The verb vidhiyate is explicitly described as 'strong and chosen: the verb of injunction and ordination', making speech the generative principle, not a passive report.

rejected_readings.label: measure_determines_essence
rejected_readings.rendering: The object's essence is determined by whether the utterance uses its own measure or another's measure; different measures yield different objects.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly uses a disjunctive (vā) to show that measure (own or another's) does not matter to the establishment: 'Whether as its own measure or as the measure of another... just so does it become established.' The establishment (rudhi) follows regardless of which measure is adopted.

notes: The verse captures the mechanism of word-dependence: speech does not describe pre-existing objects but launches and determines them, with their contours settling along the lines speech draws. The apparent tension between 'own/another's measure' and 'just so becomes established' is resolved by the disjunctive: the measure is indifferent to the establishment process, which depends solely on utterance. Contested reading (sva-matra/para-matra as phonological vs. semantic adaptation) does not affect the core claim that utterance determines object-form.
